summary:
The French Revolution of July 1830 (the July Revolution) was a three-day uprising in Paris that overthrew the ultra-royalist King Charles X and replaced him with the more moderate Louis-Philippe of the Orléans branch of the royal family, inaugurating the 'July Monarchy.' It demonstrated both the instability of the Bourbon Restoration established at the [[event:2523|Congress of Vienna]] and the continuing power of Parisian street politics to determine French governance.

Charles X had pushed increasingly toward absolute monarchy, culminating in the July Ordinances of 26 July 1830, which dissolved the newly elected Chamber of Deputies, restricted the franchise, and imposed censorship on the press. The response was immediate: Parisian workers, students, and bourgeois liberals took to the streets on 27 July in the 'Three Glorious Days' (Les Trois Glorieuses), erecting barricades and fighting royal troops. By 29 July the Tuileries had fallen and Charles X had fled Paris.

The outcome was a compromise between republican and constitutional monarchist factions. Rather than re-establishing a republic, liberal politicians offered the throne to Louis-Philippe, Duke of Orléans, whose family had supported the Revolution and who represented a more constitutional vision of monarchy. The new regime strengthened the constitutional charter, expanded the franchise slightly, and adopted the tricolor flag, but remained a constitutional monarchy rather than a republic.

The July Revolution inspired liberal and nationalist uprisings across Europe, including the [[event:2605|Belgian Revolution]], the Polish November Uprising against Russia, and disturbances in Germany and Italy. It established an important precedent: that popular insurrection could successfully overturn a government even in post-Napoleonic conservative Europe — a precedent that would resonate powerfully in the [[event:2532|Revolutions of 1848]].
